msItem.p: Fragment of a Roman inscription. The photograph appears to preserve the letters ANNI; the reading remains provisional because the surface is worn and the fragment is broken at both ends.

support.p: Small marble fragment bearing part of one line of an inscription.

condition.p: Single fragment broken on all sides; only a short sequence of letters is preserved.

layout.p: One preserved line of large capital letters.

handNote.p: Inscribed Roman capitals; reading from the photograph is uncertain, but the preserved letters more plausibly read ANNI.

summary.note: Roman marble inscription fragment, Harvard Art Museums object 1920.44.139.

origin.placeName: Roman world, exact place of origin unknown
origin.note: Roman Imperial period, Middle; Roman culture.

provenance.p: Owned by Miss Elizabeth Gaskell Norton, Boston, MA, and Miss Margaret Norton, Cambridge, MA, by 1920.
Gift of the Misses Norton to the Fogg Museum in 1920.
provenance.note: The Misses Norton were daughters of Charles Elliot Norton (1827-1908).

bibl.ref: Harvard University Art Museums web entry

listBibl.bibl: Susanne Ebbinghaus, ed., Ancient Bronzes through a Modern Lens: Introductory Essays on the Study of Ancient Mediterranean and Near Eastern Bronzes, Harvard Art Museum and Yale University Press (Cambridge, MA, 2014), p. 121.

xi:fallback.p: Taxonomies for US Epigraphy controlled values

revisionDesc.change: Created from the Harvard Art Museums PDF record using MA.Camb.HU.Sack.L.1932.56.129.v as the template; inscription read from the photograph.

surface.desc: Fragment of an Inscription, Harvard Art Museums 1920.44.139; photo © President and Fellows of Harvard College.

div.p: Fragmentary text preserving the sequence [...]ANNI[...]. No secure translation is possible; the letters could belong to a word such as anni or annis, or to a longer name or formula.

The museum record does not provide a transcription of the inscription. Re-examination of the photograph suggests that the visible sequence is better read as ANNI: the first complete letter appears to have two oblique strokes converging above, rather than a simple L; the following strokes fit two successive Ns and a final I. The breaks at both ends and the worn surface make the reading provisional.

The Harvard Art Museums record dates the object to the 1st-3rd century CE and identifies it as Roman, Roman Imperial period, Middle. The record was created from historic documentation and may be incomplete.
Reading from photograph: ANNI; all letters uncertain.
